name: Biochemistry
description: This course studies **the chemical processes of living organisms**. It explains how biomolecules are built, how cells obtain and use energy, and how biochemical pathways regulate life.

name: Biology Foundations
description: This course introduces the **fundamental principles of biology**, the science of life. Students learn what living organisms are, how they are classified, and the core concepts that underpin all of biology.

name: Biotechnology
description: This course studies **the application of biological systems and living organisms to develop technologies and products** for medicine, agriculture, industry, and environmental science.

name: Cell Biology
description: This course studies the **cell**, the fundamental unit of life. Students learn how cells are structured, how they function, how they communicate, and how they divide to support growth, repair, and reproduction.

name: Ecology
description: This course studies **how organisms interact with each other and their environment**, from individual organisms to the entire biosphere. It explains ecosystems, biodiversity, climate, and conservation.

name: Evolution
description: This course studies **how life changes over time**, explaining the origin of biological diversity through natural selection, genetic variation, speciation, and evolutionary processes.

name: Genetics
description: This course studies **how traits are inherited, how genes are passed between generations, and how genetic variation shapes living organisms**. It forms the bridge between molecular biology and evolution.

name: Human Physiology
description: This course studies **how the human body functions**. It explains how organs and organ systems work together to maintain life and homeostasis.

name: Immunology
description: This course studies **the immune system**, how it protects the body from pathogens, how vaccines work, and why immune disorders such as allergies, autoimmune diseases, and immunodeficiencies occur.

name: Microbiology
description: This course studies **microorganisms**—organisms too small to be seen with the naked eye. It covers bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, parasites, and their roles in health, disease, biotechnology, and the environment.

name: Molecular Biology
description: This course studies **how genetic information is stored, replicated, expressed, and regulated**. It forms the foundation of genetics, biotechnology, genomics, and modern medicine.

name: Acids & Bases
description: This course studies **acid-base chemistry**, one of the most important areas of chemistry. It explains pH, neutralization, buffers, titrations, and the behavior of acids and bases in biological, environmental, and industrial systems.

name: Analytical Chemistry
description: This course studies **how chemists identify, separate, and quantify chemical substances**. It is the foundation of laboratory science, pharmaceuticals, environmental testing, forensic science, and quality control.

name: Atomic Structure
description: This course explains **what atoms are made of**, how electrons are arranged, and how modern atomic theory developed. It is the foundation of all chemistry.

name: Biomolecules
description: This course studies the **chemical molecules of life**. It bridges chemistry and biology, explaining the structure, function, and interactions of carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, nucleic acids, enzymes, vitamins, and biomolecular metabolism.

name: Chemical Bonding
description: This is one of the most important chemistry courses. It explains **why atoms bond, how molecules form, and why substances have different physical and chemical properties**.

name: Chemical Equilibrium
description: Chemical Equilibrium explains **why most chemical reactions never go to completion** and how reaction conditions affect the balance between reactants and products.

name: Chemical Kinetics
description: This course studies **how fast chemical reactions occur, what controls their speed, and how reactions proceed step by step**. It is fundamental to chemistry, biology, pharmaceuticals, and chemical engineering.

name: Chemistry Foundations
description: This course teaches the language, measurements, and core principles of chemistry before students study atoms, molecules, and reactions.

name: Computational Chemistry
description: This course studies **how computers are used to model, simulate, predict, and design chemical systems**. It combines chemistry, mathematics, physics, computer science, and artificial intelligence to solve problems that are difficult or impossible to study experimentally.

name: Coordination Chemistry
description: This course studies **coordination compounds (metal complexes)**—molecules in which a central metal atom or ion is surrounded by ligands. It is a major branch of inorganic chemistry with applications in medicine, catalysis, biology, and materials science.

name: Electrochemistry
description: This course studies **the relationship between chemical reactions and electricity**. It forms the foundation of batteries, fuel cells, electrolysis, corrosion, and modern energy storage.

name: Environmental Chemistry
description: This course studies **the chemistry of Earth's environment**, including the atmosphere, water, soil, pollution, climate change, and sustainable chemical practices. It connects chemistry with ecology, public health, and environmental engineering.

name: Industrial Chemistry
description: This course studies **how chemistry is applied in large-scale manufacturing**. It covers chemical process industries, industrial production methods, process optimization, quality control, sustainability, and safety.

name: Main Group Chemistry
description: This course studies the **chemistry of the main group elements (Groups 1, 2, and 13–18)**, including their properties, compounds, reactions, and industrial importance.

name: Materials Chemistry
description: This course studies **how the composition, structure, and bonding of materials determine their properties and applications**. It bridges chemistry, physics, engineering, and nanotechnology.

name: Nanochemistry
description: This course studies **chemistry at the nanoscale (1–100 nm)**, where materials exhibit unique physical, chemical, optical, electrical, and biological properties. It is foundational to nanotechnology, medicine, electronics, and advanced materials.

name: Nuclear Chemistry
description: This course studies **the chemistry of atomic nuclei**, radioactive materials, nuclear reactions, and their applications in medicine, energy, industry, and scientific research.

name: Organic Chemistry I
description: This course introduces the **chemistry of carbon compounds**. Students learn how organic molecules are structured, named, classified, and how they react. It forms the foundation for pharmaceuticals, biochemistry, polymers, and materials science.

name: Organic Chemistry II
description: This course builds on Organic Chemistry I by exploring the **chemistry of functional groups**, reaction mechanisms, organic synthesis, and spectroscopic identification. It is the core course for pharmaceuticals, medicinal chemistry, and chemical synthesis.

name: Periodic Table
description: This course explains **why the Periodic Table is organized the way it is** and how it predicts the physical and chemical behavior of every element.

name: Polymers
description: This course studies **polymers—large molecules made of repeating units (monomers)**. It covers natural and synthetic polymers, polymer synthesis, properties, processing, and applications in materials science, medicine, and engineering.

name: Spectroscopy
description: This course studies **how light and electromagnetic radiation interact with matter** to identify atoms, molecules, and materials. It is one of the most important tools in chemistry, biology, medicine, and materials science.

name: States of Matter
description: This course explains **why matter exists as solids, liquids, gases, and plasma**, and how temperature, pressure, and intermolecular forces determine their behavior.

name: Thermochemistry
description: This course studies **how energy changes during chemical reactions**. It is one of the core subjects of physical chemistry and explains why reactions release or absorb heat.

name: Transition Metals
description: This course studies the **d-block and f-block elements**, their unique properties, variable oxidation states, colored compounds, magnetic behavior, catalytic activity, and industrial importance.

name: Algorithms
description: This course studies **how to design, analyze, and optimize algorithms** for solving computational problems efficiently. It builds directly on Data Structures.

name: Artificial Intelligence
description: This course studies **how intelligent systems perceive, reason, learn, and make decisions**. It covers the core ideas of AI beyond machine learning, including search, planning, reasoning, knowledge representation, robotics, and modern generative AI.

name: Compiler Design
description: This course studies **how programming languages are translated into executable programs**. It covers the complete compiler pipeline, from source code to optimized machine code.

name: Computer Graphics
description: This course studies **how computers create, manipulate, and render images and 3D scenes**. It covers the mathematics, algorithms, and GPU techniques behind games, simulations, CAD software, and visual effects.

name: Computer Networks
description: This course studies **how computers communicate over networks**, from small local networks to the global Internet. It covers network architecture, protocols, routing, switching, wireless communication, and network security.

name: Computer Organization
description: This course studies **how a computer is built and how it executes programs**, bridging the gap between software and hardware.

name: Computer Science Foundations
description: This course introduces **the fundamental ideas of computer science**, including how computers represent information, solve problems, execute programs, and impact society. It prepares students for programming and all later CS courses.

name: Cybersecurity
description: This course studies **how to protect computers, networks, applications, and data from cyber threats**. It covers defensive security, secure system design, incident response, and ethical security practices.

name: Data Science
description: This course studies **how to extract knowledge, insights, and predictions from data** using statistics, programming, machine learning, and visualization.

name: Data Structures
description: This course studies **how data is organized, stored, and manipulated efficiently**. Choosing the right data structure is fundamental to writing fast, scalable software.

name: Databases
description: This course studies **how data is stored, organized, queried, and managed efficiently**. It covers relational databases, SQL, NoSQL, transactions, indexing, and modern database systems.

name: Distributed Systems
description: This course studies **how multiple computers work together as one system**. It covers communication, coordination, consistency, fault tolerance, and the design of large-scale distributed applications.

name: Machine Learning
description: This course studies **how computers learn patterns from data** to make predictions, classifications, recommendations, and decisions. It covers both classical machine learning and modern deep learning.

name: Mobile Development
description: This course teaches **how to build mobile applications** for Android and iOS, covering native concepts, cross-platform development, networking, storage, and publishing.

name: Object-Oriented Programming
description: This course teaches the **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** paradigm, where software is modeled as interacting objects. The concepts apply across Java, C++, C#, Kotlin, Swift, Python, and many modern languages.

name: Operating Systems
description: This course studies **how an operating system manages computer hardware and software resources**. It explains how modern operating systems run programs, manage memory, coordinate devices, and provide secure, efficient execution.

name: Programming Fundamentals
description: This course teaches **how to write programs to solve problems**. Students learn programming concepts that apply to almost every programming language (Python, Java, C++, JavaScript, Go, Rust, etc.).

name: Software Engineering
description: This course studies **how to design, build, test, deploy, and maintain software systems** using engineering principles and industry best practices.

name: System Design
description: This course studies **how to design scalable, reliable, and maintainable software systems**. It focuses on architecture rather than implementation and prepares students to build applications that can serve thousands or millions of users.

name: Web Development
description: This course teaches **how modern websites and web applications are built**, covering both frontend and backend development, databases, APIs, authentication, and deployment.

name: Behavioral Economics
description: This course studies **how psychology influences economic decision-making**. It explains why people often make choices that differ from the predictions of traditional economics.

name: Consumer Theory
description: This course studies **how consumers make decisions** under limited income, explaining how preferences, prices, and budgets determine demand for goods and services.

name: Development Economics
description: This course studies **how economies grow and develop over time**, focusing on poverty reduction, inequality, industrialization, human development, and sustainable economic progress.

name: Econometrics
description: This course studies **how statistical and mathematical methods are used to analyze economic data**, test economic theories, estimate relationships, and make forecasts.

name: Economics Foundations
description: This course introduces **the fundamental principles of economics**, explaining how individuals, businesses, and governments make decisions under conditions of scarcity.

name: International Economics
description: This course studies **how countries trade, invest, and interact economically**. It explains the principles of international trade, exchange rates, trade policies, and the global financial system.

name: Macroeconomics
description: This course studies **the economy as a whole**, including national income, inflation, unemployment, economic growth, and the policies governments and central banks use to stabilize the economy.

name: Market Structures
description: This course studies **how different types of markets influence competition, pricing, production, and consumer welfare**. It examines how firms behave under different competitive conditions.

name: Microeconomics
description: This course studies **how individuals, households, and firms make decisions** and how these decisions determine prices, production, and resource allocation in markets.

name: Monetary Economics
description: This course studies **money, banking, financial systems, and monetary policy**. It explains how money is created, how central banks influence the economy, and how financial stability is maintained.

name: Producer Theory
description: This course studies **how firms make production decisions**, combining labor, capital, and technology to produce goods and services while minimizing costs and maximizing profits.

name: Public Economics
description: This course studies **the role of government in the economy**, including taxation, public spending, regulation, and policies designed to improve economic efficiency and social welfare.

name: Applications of Game Theory
description: This capstone course studies **how game theory is applied across economics, business, politics, biology, computer science, artificial intelligence, and everyday decision-making**. It integrates the concepts from the previous courses through real-world systems and case studies.

name: Auction Theory
description: This course studies **how auctions allocate goods and determine prices through strategic bidding**. It explores bidder behavior, auction design, and the economics behind modern marketplaces.

name: Cooperative Game Theory
description: This course studies **how groups of players cooperate to achieve shared goals and fairly distribute the resulting benefits or costs**. It introduces coalition formation, bargaining, fairness, and cooperative decision-making.

name: Evolutionary Game Theory
description: This course studies **how strategies evolve over time within populations**, rather than assuming perfectly rational players. It explains how cooperation, competition, and adaptation emerge in biology, economics, sociology, and artificial intelligence.

name: Game Theory Foundations
description: This course introduces **the science of strategic decision-making**. Students learn how rational individuals, organizations, and nations make decisions when the outcome depends on the actions of others.

name: Mechanism Design
description: This course studies **how to design rules, incentives, and institutions that produce desirable outcomes when individuals act in their own self-interest**. Unlike game theory, which analyzes existing games, mechanism design **creates the game**.

name: Nash Equilibrium
description: This course studies **Nash Equilibrium**, the central concept of game theory. It explains how stable outcomes emerge when every player chooses the best strategy given the choices of everyone else.

name: Repeated Games
description: This course studies **games that are played multiple times**, showing how repeated interactions can lead to cooperation, trust, reputation, and long-term strategic behavior.

name: Sequential Games
description: This course studies **games in which players make decisions over time**, observing previous actions before making their own choices. It introduces game trees, backward induction, and dynamic strategic reasoning.

name: Simultaneous Games
description: This course studies **games in which all players choose their strategies at the same time**, without knowing the decisions of the other players. It introduces the core analytical tools of classical game theory.

name: Strategic Decision Making
description: This course studies **how rational agents make strategic decisions** when outcomes depend on uncertainty, risk, and the choices of others. It develops the analytical tools used in economics, AI, business, politics, and negotiation.

name: Voting & Social Choice
description: This course studies **how groups combine individual preferences into collective decisions**. It explores voting systems, fairness, strategic voting, and the mathematical limits of collective decision-making.

name: Abstract Algebra
description: Abstract Algebra studies **algebraic structures** such as groups, rings, and fields. It is foundational for modern mathematics, cryptography, coding theory, and theoretical computer science.

name: Algebra I
description: Learn and master the concepts of Algebra I with structured lessons, interactive visualizations, and practice exercises.

name: Algebra II
description: This builds on Algebra I and prepares students for Precalculus and Calculus.

name: Arithmetic
description: Learn and master the concepts of Arithmetic with structured lessons, interactive visualizations, and practice exercises.

name: Calculus I
description: This is the first true calculus course.

name: Calculus II
description: Calculus II focuses on **advanced integration, sequences & series, and parametric/polar calculus**.

name: Calculus III (Multivariable Calculus)
description: This course extends calculus from **one variable** to **multiple variables**.

name: Complex Analysis
description: This is the study of **functions of complex numbers**. It is one of the most elegant areas of mathematics and has applications in physics, electrical engineering, fluid dynamics, signal processing, and quantum mechanics.

name: Differential Equations
description: This course studies equations involving derivatives and models real-world systems such as population growth, circuits, mechanics, and control systems.

name: Discrete Mathematics
description: This is the foundation of **Computer Science**, **Algorithms**, **Cryptography**, and **Logic**.

name: Geometry
description: Learn and master the concepts of Geometry with structured lessons, interactive visualizations, and practice exercises.

name: Graph Theory
description: Graph Theory is one of the most important subjects in **Computer Science, AI, Networking, Optimization, and Mathematics**.

name: Linear Algebra
description: This is one of the most important mathematics courses for engineering, computer science, AI, graphics, robotics, physics, and data science.

name: Number Theory
description: Number Theory is the study of **integers**. It is fundamental to **cryptography**, **computer science**, and **pure mathematics**.

name: Pre-Algebra
description: This is where students transition from arithmetic to algebraic thinking.

name: Precalculus
description: This course bridges **Algebra II + Trigonometry → Calculus I**.

name: Probability
description: This course is the mathematical foundation for **Statistics, Machine Learning, AI, Data Science, Finance, Risk Analysis, and Scientific Research**.

name: Real Analysis
description: This is the course where mathematics becomes **rigorous**. Instead of just calculating derivatives and integrals, students prove *why* calculus works.

name: Statistics
description: This is the standard university-level Statistics course and follows naturally after Probability.

name: Trigonometry
description: Learn and master the concepts of Trigonometry with structured lessons, interactive visualizations, and practice exercises.

name: Aesthetics
description: This course studies **the philosophy of beauty, art, and aesthetic experience**. It explores what makes something beautiful, what distinguishes art, how creativity works, and how people interpret artistic works.

name: Contemporary Philosophy
description: This course studies **the major philosophical movements, ideas, and debates from the late 19th century to the present**. It examines how philosophy addresses modern issues in science, technology, society, politics, and human existence.

name: Epistemology
description: This course studies **the nature, sources, limits, and justification of knowledge**. It explores questions about truth, belief, evidence, skepticism, and how humans acquire reliable knowledge.

name: Ethics
description: This course studies **moral philosophy**, exploring what makes actions right or wrong, what people ought to do, and how ethical principles apply to personal life, society, science, business, and technology.

name: History of Philosophy
description: This course studies **the development of philosophical thought across different civilizations and historical periods**. It examines the major philosophers, schools of thought, and the evolution of philosophical ideas.

name: Logic
description: This course studies **the principles of correct reasoning and argumentation**. It covers both informal and formal logic, providing the tools to evaluate arguments, identify fallacies, and construct rigorous proofs.

name: Metaphysics
description: This course studies **the fundamental nature of reality, existence, and being**. It explores questions about what exists, how things persist through time, the nature of causation, and the structure of reality itself.

name: Philosophy Foundations
description: This course introduces **the study of fundamental questions about reality, knowledge, morality, reasoning, and human existence**. It provides the conceptual foundation for all later philosophy courses.

name: Philosophy of Language
description: This course studies **the nature of language, meaning, and communication**. It explores how words refer to the world, how meaning is constructed, and how language relates to thought, logic, and reality.

name: Philosophy of Mind
description: This course studies **the nature of the mind, consciousness, and mental states**, exploring how thoughts, emotions, perception, and intelligence relate to the brain, the body, and artificial systems.

name: Philosophy of Science
description: This course studies **the nature of scientific knowledge, scientific reasoning, and the methods used to investigate the natural world**. It explores what distinguishes science from non-science and how scientific knowledge evolves.

name: Political Philosophy
description: This course studies **the philosophical foundations of politics, government, law, and society**. It explores questions about justice, liberty, equality, rights, authority, and the ideal political order.

name: Astrophysics
description: Astrophysics applies the laws of physics to understand **stars, planets, galaxies, black holes, and the large-scale universe**. It combines mechanics, electromagnetism, thermodynamics, relativity, quantum mechanics, and nuclear physics.

name: Atomic & Nuclear Physics
description: This course explores the structure of atoms, atomic nuclei, radioactivity, nuclear reactions, and the principles behind nuclear energy and radiation.

name: Cosmology
description: Cosmology is the study of the **origin, evolution, structure, and ultimate fate of the Universe**. It brings together everything learned in physics—mechanics, relativity, quantum mechanics, particle physics, and astrophysics.

name: Electrical Circuits
description: This course focuses on **analyzing and designing electrical circuits**. While Electromagnetism explains *why* electricity behaves the way it does, this course teaches *how to analyze real electrical networks*.

name: Electricity (Electrostatics)
description: This course focuses on **electric charge and electric fields**. It does **not** cover electric circuits—that comes later.

name: Electromagnetism
description: This course studies **electric and magnetic fields, their interactions, and the unified theory of electromagnetism**. Students learn the principles governing electric charges, magnetic fields, electromagnetic induction, and electromagnetic waves.

name: Fluid Mechanics
description: This course studies **liquids and gases at rest and in motion**. It is fundamental to civil engineering, mechanical engineering, aerospace, medicine, and environmental science.

name: Gravitation
description: This course studies gravity—from falling objects on Earth to planetary motion, satellites, and orbital mechanics.

name: Kinematics
description: Kinematics is the study of **motion without considering the forces that cause it**.

name: Magnetism
description: This course studies **magnetic fields, magnetic forces, and magnetic materials**. It builds directly on electrostatics and prepares students for electromagnetism.

name: Modern Physics
description: This course studies **the revolutionary discoveries of twentieth-century physics that transformed our understanding of matter, energy, space, and time**.

name: Momentum & Collisions
description: This course studies interactions between objects using **momentum**, which is essential for mechanics, engineering, astronomy, and particle physics.

name: Newtonian Mechanics
description: This course explains **why objects move** by introducing forces and Newton's Laws.

name: Optics
description: This course studies **the behavior of light**, from mirrors and lenses to interference, diffraction, polarization, lasers, and fiber optics.

name: Oscillations & Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)
description: This course studies **repetitive motion** and forms the foundation for **waves, sound, electronics, and quantum mechanics**.

name: Particle Physics
description: Particle Physics studies the **fundamental particles and forces** that make up the universe. It is the foundation of modern high-energy physics.

name: Physics Foundations
description: This course teaches the language of physics before students learn mechanics.

name: Plasma Physics
description: Plasma Physics studies the **fourth state of matter**—ionized gases. It underpins fusion energy, space physics, astrophysics, and many industrial technologies.

name: Quantum Mechanics
description: Quantum Mechanics is the mathematical framework that describes the behavior of matter and energy at atomic and subatomic scales. It is one of the most important courses in all of physics.

name: Relativity
description: This course studies **Einstein's theories of Special and General Relativity**, explaining space, time, gravity, black holes, and the structure of spacetime.

name: Rotational Motion
description: This course extends linear mechanics to rotating objects. It's one of the most important courses in classical physics and engineering.

name: Solid State Physics
description: This course studies the physics of **solids and crystalline materials**. It is the foundation of **electronics, semiconductors, nanotechnology, materials science, and modern computing**.

name: Thermodynamics
description: This course studies **heat, temperature, energy transfer, and the behavior of matter**. It is fundamental to physics, chemistry, mechanical engineering, and aerospace engineering.

name: Waves & Sound
description: This course studies how **disturbances propagate through matter and space**, covering everything from musical instruments to earthquakes and communication systems.

name: Work, Energy & Power
description: This course teaches how to analyze motion using **energy**, often making problems much simpler than using Newton's laws alone.

name: Abnormal Psychology
description: This course studies **psychological disorders**, including their causes, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment. It examines mental health through biological, psychological, and social perspectives.

name: Biological Psychology
description: This course studies **the biological basis of behavior**, exploring how the brain, nervous system, hormones, and genetics influence thoughts, emotions, learning, and behavior.

name: Clinical Psychology
description: This course studies **the assessment, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of psychological disorders**. It focuses on evidence-based clinical practice and therapeutic approaches.

name: Cognitive Psychology
description: This course studies **how the mind acquires, processes, stores, and uses information**. It explores perception, attention, memory, language, reasoning, problem solving, and decision making.

name: Developmental Psychology
description: This course studies **how people grow and change throughout the lifespan**, from conception to old age. It explores physical, cognitive, emotional, and social development.

name: Industrial & Organizational Psychology
description: This course studies **how psychology is applied in workplaces and organizations**. It explores employee behavior, leadership, teamwork, motivation, organizational culture, and workplace well-being.

name: Learning & Behavior
description: This course studies **how humans and animals acquire, modify, and maintain behavior** through experience. It covers the major theories of learning and their practical applications in education, therapy, and everyday life.

name: Personality Psychology
description: This course studies **personality**—the enduring patterns of thoughts, emotions, and behaviors that make individuals unique. It explores major theories of personality, personality assessment, development, and applications.

name: Positive Psychology
description: This course studies **the science of human flourishing**, focusing on the strengths, habits, relationships, and experiences that contribute to happiness, resilience, meaning, and well-being.

name: Psychology Foundations
description: This course introduces **the scientific study of mind and behavior**. Students learn the major branches of psychology, research methods, biological foundations, and the core concepts that underpin the field.

name: Research Methods & Statistics
description: This course studies **how psychological research is designed, conducted, analyzed, and interpreted**. It provides the scientific foundation for evaluating evidence and conducting psychological research.

name: Social Psychology
description: This course studies **how people's thoughts, feelings, and behaviors are influenced by the presence of others**. It explores social perception, attitudes, relationships, groups, and social influence.
